scenario: Vanta Health, a regional hospital network, admits 2500 records per week through Camunda 8.8 Forms. A number field on the admission form has the Key set to user.info.age, and a nurse enters 42 at the bedside, so the team must know the JSON shape the runtime produces in 3 seconds before downstream FEEL reads it.
question: Which output data structure does the runtime produce on submission of this field?

options.text: A single flat variable literally named user.info.age whose value is 42, with no nested object created in the process variables payload
An array under user containing one object info that holds the age 42 in its first element, mirroring how dynamic lists serialise their rows
A single variable whose name joins user, info, and age with underscores and holds the value 42 while the runtime collapses dotted keys to a flat name
A nested object where user contains info, and info contains age set to 42, so the path key materialises as a real nested data structure

a.text: Incorrect. Path keys are not stored as flat dotted names; the runtime materialises the dotted path into a nested object structure on submission.

b.text: Incorrect. A plain field with a path key produces a nested object, not an array; arrays are the output of dynamic lists, not of a single field.

c.text: Incorrect. The runtime does not rewrite dots into underscores; the documented behaviour is to materialise the path into nested objects.

d.text: Correct. With the Key set to user.info.age and value 42, the documented output is the nested object {"user":{"info":{"age":42}}} on submission.

explanation: Defining the Key as a path like user.info.age tells the runtime to build the matching nested object structure on submission instead of treating the dotted string as a single flat variable name.
